    10. Intent: therapy_request
    11. Agent Switch: Therapy Recommendation Agent
    12. RAG Retrieval: Finds stress + sleep therapies
    13. Recommendation: Yoga Nidra + Pranayama with rationale
    14. TTS: Converts to voice
    15. Storage: Saves conversation + recommendation to DB

    Key Features:

    ✅ Multilingual Support: English, Hindi, Hinglish ✅ Crisis Detection: Real-time safety monitoring ✅ Multi-Agent System: Specialized agents for different needs ✅ RAG Integration: Knowledge-grounded responses ✅ Voice Enabled: Full STT + TTS pipeline ✅ Persistent Memory: Long-term user understanding ✅ Scalable Architecture: Docker-based deployment

    Performance Metrics: - Sub-second response latency - 95%+ intent detection accuracy - Real-time crisis intervention - Seamless voice interaction

    This system combines conversational AI, RAG, agentic workflows, and voice technology to provide comprehensive mental wellness support.

    Role: AI Solutions Engineer¶

    Interview 1 (Possible Company context: [Company])¶

    Q: How do you ensure commitment to stay with the next company for at least a couple of years, given your short tenure at the current role?

    I completely understand the concern about my current tenure being relatively short, so let me address this directly and transparently.

    Why My Current Situation is Different:

    • My current role is contractor-based, not a full-time position — it was always intended as a project-based engagement rather than a long-term career commitment
    • The work has become saturated after 8-9 months — I've successfully built and deployed the entire chatbot system, and now it's primarily incremental maintenance work (new PDF/video ingestion)
    • There's no clear technical roadmap or growth path — I'm the only AI/ML engineer working on LLM systems, with no senior technical mentors to learn from or collaborate with on strategic initiatives
    • The lack of full-time benefits and job security inherent in contractor roles makes this fundamentally different from leaving a permanent position

    My Commitment to the Next Role:

    • I'm specifically seeking a full-time, long-term position where I can build a career, not just complete a project
    • I'm looking for a company with a clear technical vision and roadmap in AI-powered compliance and regulatory automation — areas where I can continuously learn and contribute meaningfully over years
    • I want to work with strong technical teams where I can both contribute my expertise and grow through collaboration with senior engineers and domain experts
    • My track record shows commitment when the environment is right: I stayed at [Company] for over 2 years (April 2021 - July 2023) while simultaneously pursuing my M.Tech, demonstrating my ability to commit long-term when there's mutual growth

    What I'm Looking For:

    • A role where I can build production-grade AI systems at scale for enterprise customers, not just maintain existing systems
    • Opportunities to work on challenging problems in regulatory compliance that require continuous innovation
    • A collaborative environment with clear growth paths and technical mentorship
    • Stability and long-term career development that comes with a full-time position

    I'm not looking to job-hop — I'm looking to find the right long-term fit where I can make significant contributions and grow alongside the company for years to come. [Company]'s mission in regulatory automation and the technical challenges involved are exactly the kind of meaningful work I want to commit to long-term.

    Q: Write an algorithm to parse two PDF versions of a regulation and summarize the differences between them concisely.

    Problem Understanding: I need to build a system that compares two versions of regulatory documents (PDFs) and produces a concise summary of what changed between versions — this is critical for compliance teams to quickly understand regulatory updates.

    🔑 Key Approach:

    High-Level Strategy: - Extract & chunk both PDFs with structure preservation (sections, headings) - Semantic comparison using embeddings to identify modified/added/removed sections - LLM-based diff summarization to generate human-readable change summaries - Hierarchical output organized by change type and importance

    Architecture Components: 1. PDF Parsing Layer (structure-aware extraction) 2. Semantic Chunking & Embedding (section-level comparison) 3. Diff Detection Engine (identify changes) 4. LLM Summarization (generate concise summaries)

    💻 Code Solution:

    import PyPDF2
from sentence_transformers import SentenceTransformer
from sklearn.metrics.pairwise import cosine_similarity
import numpy as np
from langchain.text_splitter import RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter
from langchain_groq import ChatGroq
from langchain.prompts import PromptTemplate
import re

class RegulationDiffAnalyzer:
    def __init__(self, model_name="all-MiniLM-L6-v2", llm_model="llama-3.3-70b-versatile"):
        # Initialize embedding model for semantic comparison
        self.embedding_model = SentenceTransformer(model_name)

        # Initialize LLM for summarization
        self.llm = ChatGroq(model=llm_model, temperature=0.1)

        # Text splitter for chunking with section awareness
        self.text_splitter = RecursiveCharacterTextSplitter(
            chunk_size=1000,
            chunk_overlap=200,
            separators=["\n\n", "\n", ". ", " "]
        )

    def extract_pdf_content(self, pdf_path):
        """Extract text content from PDF with structure preservation"""
        with open(pdf_path, 'rb') as file:
            pdf_reader = PyPDF2.PdfReader(file)
            full_text = ""

            # Extract text from all pages
            for page in pdf_reader.pages:
                full_text += page.extract_text() + "\n"

        return full_text

    def extract_sections(self, text):
        """Extract sections with headings for structured comparison"""
        # Pattern to identify section headings (numbers, letters, or bold text patterns)
        section_pattern = r'(\d+\.?\d*\.?\s+[A-Z][^\n]+|[A-Z\s]{3,}(?=\n))'

        sections = []
        current_section = {"heading": "Introduction", "content": ""}

        # Split by section headings
        parts = re.split(section_pattern, text)

        for i, part in enumerate(parts):
            if i % 2 == 1:  # Heading
                if current_section["content"].strip():
                    sections.append(current_section)
                current_section = {"heading": part.strip(), "content": ""}
            else:  # Content
                current_section["content"] += part

        # Add last section
        if current_section["content"].strip():
            sections.append(current_section)

        return sections

    def compute_embeddings(self, sections):
        """Generate embeddings for each section"""
        embeddings = []
        for section in sections:
            # Combine heading and content for embedding
            text = f"{section['heading']}: {section['content'][:500]}"
            embedding = self.embedding_model.encode(text)
            embeddings.append(embedding)

        return np.array(embeddings)

    def find_matching_sections(self, sections_v1, embeddings_v1, sections_v2, embeddings_v2, threshold=0.7):
        """Find matching sections between two versions using semantic similarity"""
        matches = []
        added_sections = []
        removed_sections = []

        # Compute similarity matrix
        similarity_matrix = cosine_similarity(embeddings_v1, embeddings_v2)

        matched_v2_indices = set()

        # Find matches for v1 sections
        for i, section_v1 in enumerate(sections_v1):
            max_similarity_idx = np.argmax(similarity_matrix[i])
            max_similarity = similarity_matrix[i][max_similarity_idx]

            if max_similarity >= threshold:
                # Match found
                matches.append({
                    "v1_section": section_v1,
                    "v2_section": sections_v2[max_similarity_idx],
                    "similarity": max_similarity,
                    "v1_idx": i,
                    "v2_idx": max_similarity_idx
                })
                matched_v2_indices.add(max_similarity_idx)
            else:
                # Section removed in v2
                removed_sections.append(section_v1)

        # Find new sections in v2
        for j, section_v2 in enumerate(sections_v2):
            if j not in matched_v2_indices:
                added_sections.append(section_v2)

        return matches, added_sections, removed_sections

    def generate_diff_summary(self, matches, added_sections, removed_sections):
        """Generate concise summary of differences using LLM"""

        # Prepare modified sections for summarization
        modified_summaries = []
        for match in matches:
            if match["similarity"] < 0.95:  # Significant change threshold
                v1_content = match["v1_section"]["content"][:800]
                v2_content = match["v2_section"]["content"][:800]

                # Use LLM to summarize the change
                prompt = PromptTemplate(
                    template="""Compare these two versions of a regulation section and summarize ONLY the key changes in 2-3 concise bullet points:

**Section: {heading}**

**Version 1:**
{v1_content}

**Version 2:**
{v2_content}

Provide a concise summary of changes:""",
                    input_variables=["heading", "v1_content", "v2_content"]
                )

                response = self.llm.invoke(
                    prompt.format(
                        heading=match["v1_section"]["heading"],
                        v1_content=v1_content,
                        v2_content=v2_content
                    )
                )

                modified_summaries.append({
                    "section": match["v1_section"]["heading"],
                    "changes": response.content
                })

        # Format final summary
        final_summary = "# Regulation Comparison Summary\n\n"

        # Modified sections
        if modified_summaries:
            final_summary += "## 📝 Modified Sections\n\n"
            for item in modified_summaries:
                final_summary += f"**{item['section']}**\n{item['changes']}\n\n"

        # Added sections
        if added_sections:
            final_summary += "## ✅ New Sections Added\n\n"
            for section in added_sections:
                final_summary += f"- **{section['heading']}**: {section['content'][:200]}...\n"

        # Removed sections
        if removed_sections:
            final_summary += "\n## ❌ Sections Removed\n\n"
            for section in removed_sections:
                final_summary += f"- **{section['heading']}**\n"

        return final_summary

    def analyze_differences(self, pdf_v1_path, pdf_v2_path):
        """Main method to analyze differences between two PDF versions"""

        # Step 1: Extract content from both PDFs
        print("📄 Extracting PDF content...")
        text_v1 = self.extract_pdf_content(pdf_v1_path)
        text_v2 = self.extract_pdf_content(pdf_v2_path)

        # Step 2: Extract sections with structure
        print("🔍 Extracting sections...")
        sections_v1 = self.extract_sections(text_v1)
        sections_v2 = self.extract_sections(text_v2)

        # Step 3: Generate embeddings for semantic comparison
        print("🧠 Computing embeddings...")
        embeddings_v1 = self.compute_embeddings(sections_v1)
        embeddings_v2 = self.compute_embeddings(sections_v2)

        # Step 4: Find matching, added, and removed sections
        print("🔄 Comparing sections...")
        matches, added_sections, removed_sections = self.find_matching_sections(
            sections_v1, embeddings_v1, sections_v2, embeddings_v2
        )

        # Step 5: Generate concise summary using LLM
        print("✍️ Generating summary...")
        summary = self.generate_diff_summary(matches, added_sections, removed_sections)

        return summary

# Usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    analyzer = RegulationDiffAnalyzer()

    # Analyze the two regulation PDFs
    summary = analyzer.analyze_differences("regulation_v1.pdf", "regulation_v2.pdf")

    # Print summary
    print(summary)

    # Optionally save to file
    with open("regulation_diff_summary.md", "w") as f:
        f.write(summary)

    💡 Detailed Explanation:

    1. Architecture Design: - Hybrid approach: Combines semantic similarity (embeddings) with LLM-based summarization for accuracy and conciseness - Section-aware parsing: Preserves document structure to compare logical units rather than arbitrary chunks - Three-tier change detection: Identifies modified, added, and removed sections separately

    2. Key Technical Decisions:

    PDF Parsing: - Uses PyPDF2 for text extraction with page-by-page processing - Regex-based section detection to identify headings (numbered sections, capitalized titles) - Preserves hierarchical structure for meaningful comparisons

    Semantic Comparison: - sentence-transformers (all-MiniLM-L6-v2) generates 384-dim embeddings for each section - Cosine similarity matrix identifies matching sections across versions - Threshold of 0.7 balances precision/recall for section matching - Similarity < 0.95 flags sections as "modified" even if matched

    LLM Summarization: - Groq Llama 3.3-70B with low temperature (0.1) for factual, consistent summaries - Prompt engineering focuses on "key changes only" to ensure conciseness - Limits input to 800 chars per section to stay within context windows

    3. Scalability Considerations: - For very large PDFs (100+ pages), implement chunking with overlap and batch embedding generation - Use vector databases (FAISS/Qdrant) for faster similarity search on large document sets - Add caching layer for embeddings to avoid recomputation - Implement parallel processing for multi-document comparisons

    4. Output Quality: - Hierarchical summary: Organized by change type (modified/added/removed) - Concise bullet points: LLM generates 2-3 key changes per section - Markdown formatting: Easy to read and export to reports

    5. Time Complexity: - PDF extraction: O(n) where n = number of pages - Embedding generation: O(m) where m = number of sections - Similarity computation: O(m₁ × m₂) for comparing sections - LLM summarization: O(k) where k = number of modified sections - Overall: O(n + m₁×m₂ + k) — efficient for typical regulatory documents

    6. Potential Enhancements: - Add table/figure comparison using multimodal models (ColPali, GPT-4V) - Implement change importance scoring to prioritize critical updates - Add version control integration for tracking changes over time - Build interactive UI for exploring differences section-by-section

    This solution provides a production-ready framework for regulatory document comparison that balances accuracy, conciseness, and scalability — directly applicable to [Company]'s compliance automation use cases.

    Q: Can you elaborate on what exactly you mean by semantic chunking and how you will implement it?

    Absolutely! Let me explain my semantic chunking strategy for this regulatory document comparison problem.

    Core Concept: Instead of using fixed-size chunking (e.g., every 512 tokens), I'll use structure-aware semantic chunking that respects the logical boundaries of the regulatory document — preserving complete sections, subsections, and paragraphs as coherent units.

    Why This Matters for Regulatory Documents: - Regulatory documents have hierarchical structure (sections like "Scope", "Definitions", "Requirements", "Compliance") - A regulation clause or requirement often spans multiple sentences and must be kept together to preserve legal meaning - Splitting mid-section would break context and make comparison inaccurate

    My Implementation Approach:

    Step 1: Structure-Aware Extraction - Use regex patterns to identify section headings: - Numbered sections: "1.0 Introduction", "2.3.1 Requirements" - Capitalized headings: "SCOPE", "DEFINITIONS" - Bold/formatted text patterns - Parse the document into a hierarchical tree of sections and subsections

    Step 2: Semantic Boundary Detection - Create chunks at natural semantic boundaries: - Section breaks (highest priority) - Paragraph breaks (\n\n) - Sentence boundaries (only if section is too large) - Each chunk represents a complete logical unit (e.g., one full requirement clause)

    Step 3: Chunk Size Management - Set max chunk size (e.g., 1000 characters) to stay within embedding model limits - If a section exceeds this, split at paragraph boundaries within that section - Add 200-character overlap between adjacent chunks to preserve context at boundaries

    Step 4: Metadata Preservation - Each chunk stores: - Section heading (e.g., "3.2 Data Protection Requirements") - Hierarchy level (section, subsection, paragraph) - Page number and position in document - Chunk ID for tracking

    Example from Regulatory Document:

    Section 2.1: Data Retention Requirements
Organizations must retain customer data for a minimum of 7 years...
[Complete 800-character section kept as ONE chunk]

Section 2.2: Data Deletion Procedures  
When the retention period expires, organizations shall...
[Another complete chunk]

    Why This Beats Fixed Chunking: - Preserves legal context: A compliance requirement isn't split across chunks - Better semantic matching: When comparing versions, I can match "Section 2.1" in v1 to "Section 2.1" in v2 directly - Accurate change detection: If Section 2.1 changed, I know exactly what changed without losing context

    Implementation in My Code: 

    def extract_sections(self, text):
    # Regex to find section headings
    section_pattern = r'(\d+\.?\d*\.?\s+[A-Z][^\n]+|[A-Z\s]{3,}(?=\n))'

    sections = []
    current_section = {"heading": "Introduction", "content": ""}

    # Split by semantic boundaries (section headings)
    parts = re.split(section_pattern, text)

    for i, part in enumerate(parts):
        if i % 2 == 1:  # This is a heading
            if current_section["content"].strip():
                sections.append(current_section)
            current_section = {"heading": part.strip(), "content": ""}
        else:  # This is content
            current_section["content"] += part

    return sections

    Embedding Strategy: - Generate embeddings for each semantic chunk (section + content) - Use sentence-transformers (e.g., all-MiniLM-L6-v2) to create 384-dim vectors - Compare chunks using cosine similarity to find matching sections across versions

    Benefits for This Task: - Accurate section matching: "Section 3.1" in v1 maps to "Section 3.1" in v2 even if wording changed - Precise change detection: I can identify if a section was modified, added, or removed - Concise summaries: LLM only summarizes sections that actually changed, not arbitrary text fragments

    This approach is production-ready and directly applicable to [Company]'s compliance document analysis use cases!

    Q: How does PyMuPDF help with semantic chunking? The interviewer is unclear on how text extraction relates to semantic chunking.

    Let me clarify the connection between PyMuPDF and semantic chunking — I realize I need to explain this more clearly.

    The Key Point: PyMuPDF isn't just for text extraction — it provides font metadata (font size, font weight, bounding boxes) that allows me to identify document structure, which is essential for semantic chunking.

    How PyMuPDF Enables Semantic Chunking:

    Step 1: Extract Text WITH Metadata 

    import fitz  # PyMuPDF

doc = fitz.open("regulation.pdf")
for page in doc:
    blocks = page.get_text("dict")["blocks"]

    for block in blocks:
        text = block["text"]
        font_size = block["font_size"]      # e.g., 16pt vs 12pt
        font_weight = block["font_weight"]  # e.g., bold vs regular
        bbox = block["bbox"]                # position on page

    Step 2: Use Font Metadata to Identify Structure - Headings: Larger font size (e.g., 16pt) + bold weight - Section numbers: Pattern like "1.0", "2.3.1" + larger font - Body text: Regular font size (12pt) + normal weight - Subsections: Medium font size (14pt)

    Example Detection Logic: 

    def classify_block(block):
    if block["font_size"] >= 16 and "bold" in block["font_weight"]:
        return "HEADING"
    elif re.match(r'^\d+\.', block["text"]) and block["font_size"] >= 14:
        return "SECTION_NUMBER"
    else:
        return "BODY_TEXT"

    Step 3: Build Semantic Chunks Based on Structure 

    sections = []
current_section = {"heading": "", "content": ""}

for block in all_blocks:
    block_type = classify_block(block)

    if block_type == "HEADING":
        # Save previous section
        if current_section["content"]:
            sections.append(current_section)

        # Start new section
        current_section = {
            "heading": block["text"],
            "content": ""
        }
    else:
        # Add to current section's content
        current_section["content"] += block["text"] + "\n"

    Why This Matters:

    Without PyMuPDF (using basic PDF extractors): 

    "1.0 Introduction Organizations must comply with data protection 
regulations. 2.0 Scope This regulation applies to all entities..."
    → Just a flat text blob — no way to know where sections start/end

    With PyMuPDF (using font metadata): 

    Section 1: "1.0 Introduction" (font_size=16, bold)
  Content: "Organizations must comply with data protection regulations."

Section 2: "2.0 Scope" (font_size=16, bold)  
  Content: "This regulation applies to all entities..."
    → Structured chunks that preserve semantic boundaries

    The Complete Flow:

    1. PyMuPDF extracts text + font metadata → I know which text is a heading vs body
    2. I use font metadata to detect section boundaries → Build hierarchical structure
    3. I create semantic chunks at section boundaries → Each chunk = complete logical unit
    4. I generate embeddings for each semantic chunk → Compare sections across versions

    Why Not Just Use Regex on Plain Text? - Regulatory documents often have inconsistent formatting (some use "1.0", others use "Section 1", others use "I.") - Font metadata is more reliable than text patterns alone - PyMuPDF preserves mathematical symbols and special characters that other libraries corrupt

    In Summary: PyMuPDF gives me the structural information (font sizes, weights) needed to automatically detect where sections begin and end, which is the foundation for creating semantically meaningful chunks rather than arbitrary fixed-size chunks.

    Does this clarify how PyMuPDF enables semantic chunking for this regulatory document comparison task?

    Q: If LLMs can help generate this solution in 45 minutes to 1 hour, what differentiates you from others who have the same access? How do you ensure this isn't easily replicated?

    This is an excellent question that gets to the heart of what makes an experienced AI engineer valuable in the LLM era. Let me address this directly:

    The Key Differentiators:

    1. System Design & Architecture Knowledge - What LLMs can't do: LLMs generate code snippets, but they don't understand production requirements — scalability, cost optimization, error handling, edge cases - What I bring: I know that for regulatory documents at scale, this approach needs: - Caching layer to avoid re-processing unchanged sections (saves 80%+ API costs) - Batch processing for comparing 100+ document versions simultaneously - Version control integration to track regulatory changes over time - Confidence scoring to flag uncertain matches for human review - Example from my work: In the Calming AI project, I built a symptom-change cache that reduced API costs to near-zero for repeated queries — this wasn't in any LLM-generated code, it came from understanding production constraints

    2. Domain-Specific Optimization - What LLMs generate: Generic RAG pipelines that work "okay" for everything - What I build: Domain-optimized systems that understand regulatory document structure: - Regulatory documents have legal cross-references ("as defined in Section 2.1") — my system would need to resolve these before comparison - Compliance requirements have hierarchical dependencies — a change in Section 2 might invalidate Section 5 - Version numbering isn't always sequential — need fuzzy matching for section alignment - Example: The code I just wrote uses similarity thresholds (0.7, 0.95) — these aren't arbitrary; they're based on understanding that regulatory text has high semantic overlap but critical word-level differences

    3. Debugging & Production Readiness - What happened in this session: The LLM-generated code had multiple bugs (section_match not defined, section_difference errors) - What I did: I debugged and fixed these issues in real-time because I understand: - Python data structures and scope - How the alignment algorithm works - Where to add error handling - In production: When this system fails at 2 AM because a PDF has an unexpected table format, I can diagnose and fix it — someone without deep knowledge would be stuck

    4. Cost & Performance Engineering - What the LLM-generated code does: Calls the LLM API for every single section comparison (54 sections = 54 API calls = high cost) - What I would optimize: - Batch similar sections into one API call (reduce to 5-10 calls) - Use embeddings first to filter out unchanged sections (only LLM-summarize the 10-20% that changed) - Implement tiered summarization (detailed for critical sections, brief for minor changes) - Real impact: This could reduce API costs from $50/comparison to $5/comparison at scale

    5. Evaluation & Quality Assurance - What's missing from this code: How do we know the summary is accurate? - What I would add: - Ground truth validation against manually annotated regulatory changes - Hallucination detection to ensure the LLM isn't inventing differences - Compliance team feedback loop to iteratively improve summary quality - Example from my work: In the Calming AI RAG system, I achieved 90+ similarity scores and 80% groundedness through systematic evaluation and iteration

    6. Integration & Productionization - What the LLM gives: A standalone Python script - What a production system needs: - FastAPI endpoints for real-time comparison requests - Async processing for large document batches - Database integration to store comparison history - User authentication and access control - Monitoring & alerting for system health - CI/CD pipeline for safe deployments - My experience: I've deployed production systems with Docker, Kubernetes, Redis, PostgreSQL — this isn't something LLMs can architect

    7. Strategic Problem-Solving - What happened in this interview: I made strategic decisions: - Use PyMuPDF for font metadata (not just text extraction) - Implement semantic chunking at section boundaries (not fixed-size) - Use similarity thresholds to detect modifications (not just exact matches) - Chose Groq for cost-effective LLM calls - These decisions came from experience, not from prompting an LLM

    The Bottom Line:

    Anyone can generate code with LLMs, but: - I know which code to generate (system design) - I know how to fix it when it breaks (debugging) - I know how to make it production-ready (scalability, cost, reliability) - I know how to evaluate if it's working (quality assurance) - I know how to optimize it for real-world constraints (performance engineering)

    Analogy: LLMs are like having a junior developer who can write code fast but doesn't understand the bigger picture. I'm the senior engineer who architects the system, reviews the code, catches the bugs, and ensures it works at scale.

    Proof from My Resume: - Built a multi-agent system with 95% intent detection accuracy — this required understanding conversation state management, not just prompting - Achieved sub-second routing latency in production — this required performance optimization, not just code generation - Reduced model training error rate to 98%+ data quality at [Company] — this required systematic data engineering, not just scripts

    This is what [Company] is paying for — not just code, but production-grade AI systems that work reliably at scale.

    Q: How would you make this PDF comparison implementation modular and production-ready?

    Great question! Let me break down how I'd architect this as a modular, scalable production system rather than a monolithic script.

    🏗️ Modular Architecture Design:

    1. Microservices Separation

    ┌─────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│                    API Gateway (FastAPI)                     │
│              • Request validation • Rate limiting            │
│              • Authentication (JWT) • Load balancing         │
└──────┬──────────────────┬──────────────────┬───────────────┘
       │                  │                  │
       ▼                  ▼                  ▼
┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────────┐
│ PDF Parser   │  │ Diff Engine  │  │ LLM Summarizer   │
│ Service      │  │ Service      │  │ Service          │
└──────────────┘  └──────────────┘  └──────────────────┘
       │                  │                  │
       ▼                  ▼                  ▼
┌──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┐
│         Shared Infrastructure Layer                   │
│  • Redis Cache • Vector DB • PostgreSQL • S3         │
└──────────────────────────────────────────────────────┘

    2. Core Modules Breakdown

    Module 1: PDF Processing Service 

    # pdf_processor/service.py
class PDFProcessorService:
    """Handles all PDF extraction and structure detection"""

    def __init__(self, cache_client: Redis):
        self.cache = cache_client
        self.parser = PyMuPDFParser()

    async def extract_structured_content(
        self, 
        pdf_path: str
    ) -> StructuredDocument:
        """Extract with caching"""
        cache_key = f"pdf:{hash(pdf_path)}"

        # Check cache first
        if cached := await self.cache.get(cache_key):
            return StructuredDocument.parse_raw(cached)

        # Extract and cache
        doc = await self.parser.extract_with_structure(pdf_path)
        await self.cache.setex(
            cache_key, 
            3600,  # 1 hour TTL
            doc.json()
        )
        return doc

    Module 2: Semantic Comparison Engine 

    # diff_engine/service.py
class DiffEngineService:
    """Handles section matching and change detection"""

    def __init__(
        self, 
        embedding_model: SentenceTransformer,
        vector_db: QdrantClient
    ):
        self.embedder = embedding_model
        self.vector_db = vector_db

    async def compute_diff(
        self,
        doc_v1: StructuredDocument,
        doc_v2: StructuredDocument
    ) -> DocumentDiff:
        """Async diff computation with vector DB"""

        # Batch embed all sections
        embeddings_v1 = await self._batch_embed(doc_v1.sections)
        embeddings_v2 = await self._batch_embed(doc_v2.sections)

        # Store in vector DB for fast similarity search
        matches = await self._find_matches_vectordb(
            embeddings_v1, 
            embeddings_v2
        )

        return DocumentDiff(
            modified=matches.modified,
            added=matches.added,
            removed=matches.removed
        )

    Module 3: LLM Summarization Service 

    # summarizer/service.py
class SummarizerService:
    """Handles LLM-based change summarization"""

    def __init__(self, llm_client: AsyncGroq):
        self.llm = llm_client
        self.rate_limiter = AsyncRateLimiter(
            max_calls=100, 
            period=60
        )

    async def summarize_changes(
        self,
        diff: DocumentDiff,
        batch_size: int = 5
    ) -> ChangeSummary:
        """Batch summarization with rate limiting"""

        summaries = []

        # Batch similar changes together
        batches = self._create_batches(
            diff.modified, 
            batch_size
        )

        # Process batches concurrently with rate limiting
        async with self.rate_limiter:
            tasks = [
                self._summarize_batch(batch) 
                for batch in batches
            ]
            summaries = await asyncio.gather(*tasks)

        return ChangeSummary(items=summaries)

    3. FastAPI Main Application

    # main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI, BackgroundTasks, Depends
from fastapi.responses import StreamingResponse
import asyncio

app = FastAPI(title="Regulatory Document Diff API")

# Dependency injection
def get_pdf_processor() -> PDFProcessorService:
    return PDFProcessorService(redis_client)

def get_diff_engine() -> DiffEngineService:
    return DiffEngineService(embedding_model, qdrant_client)

def get_summarizer() -> SummarizerService:
    return SummarizerService(groq_client)

@app.post("/api/v1/compare")
async def compare_documents(
    request: CompareRequest,
    background_tasks: BackgroundTasks,
    pdf_processor: PDFProcessorService = Depends(get_pdf_processor),
    diff_engine: DiffEngineService = Depends(get_diff_engine),
    summarizer: SummarizerService = Depends(get_summarizer)
):
    """
    Compare two regulatory documents
    Returns: Job ID for async processing
    """

    # Create job
    job_id = str(uuid.uuid4())

    # Queue background task
    background_tasks.add_task(
        process_comparison,
        job_id,
        request.pdf_v1_url,
        request.pdf_v2_url,
        pdf_processor,
        diff_engine,
        summarizer
    )

    return {"job_id": job_id, "status": "processing"}

@app.get("/api/v1/compare/{job_id}")
async def get_comparison_result(job_id: str):
    """Poll for comparison results"""
    result = await redis_client.get(f"job:{job_id}")

    if not result:
        return {"status": "processing"}

    return ChangeSummary.parse_raw(result)

@app.get("/api/v1/compare/{job_id}/stream")
async def stream_comparison(
    job_id: str,
    summarizer: SummarizerService = Depends(get_summarizer)
):
    """Stream results as they're generated"""

    async def generate():
        async for chunk in summarizer.stream_summary(job_id):
            yield f"data: {chunk.json()}\n\n"

    return StreamingResponse(
        generate(),
        media_type="text/event-stream"
    )

    4. Scalability Features

    Caching Strategy: 

    # cache/strategy.py
class CacheStrategy:
    """Multi-tier caching"""

    def __init__(self):
        self.l1_cache = TTLCache(maxsize=100, ttl=300)  # In-memory
        self.l2_cache = redis_client  # Redis
        self.l3_cache = s3_client  # S3 for large PDFs

    async def get_or_compute(
        self, 
        key: str, 
        compute_fn: Callable
    ):
        # L1: In-memory
        if key in self.l1_cache:
            return self.l1_cache[key]

        # L2: Redis
        if cached := await self.l2_cache.get(key):
            self.l1_cache[key] = cached
            return cached

        # L3: S3 (for large documents)
        if s3_cached := await self.l3_cache.get(key):
            await self.l2_cache.setex(key, 3600, s3_cached)
            return s3_cached

        # Compute and cache
        result = await compute_fn()
        await self._cache_all_tiers(key, result)
        return result

    Horizontal Scaling: 

    # deployment/docker-compose.yml
services:
  api-gateway:
    image: nginx:latest
    ports:
      - "80:80"
    depends_on:
      - fastapi-1
      - fastapi-2
      - fastapi-3

  fastapi-1:
    build: .
    environment:
      - WORKER_ID=1
    deploy:
      replicas: 3

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    deploy:
      replicas: 3  # Redis cluster

  qdrant:
    image: qdrant/qdrant:latest
    deploy:
      replicas: 2

    5. Production Features

    Error Handling & Retry Logic: 

    from tenacity import retry, stop_after_attempt, wait_exponential

@retry(
    stop=stop_after_attempt(3),
    wait=wait_exponential(multiplier=1, min=2, max=10)
)
async def call_llm_with_retry(prompt: str):
    """Retry with exponential backoff"""
    try:
        return await groq_client.chat.completions.create(...)
    except RateLimitError:
        # Wait and retry
        raise
    except AuthenticationError:
        # Don't retry auth errors
        raise ValueError("Invalid API key")

    Monitoring & Observability: 

    from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram
import structlog

# Metrics
comparison_requests = Counter(
    'comparison_requests_total',
    'Total comparison requests'
)
comparison_duration = Histogram(
    'comparison_duration_seconds',
    'Time to complete comparison'
)

# Structured logging
logger = structlog.get_logger()

@app.middleware("http")
async def log_requests(request: Request, call_next):
    start_time = time.time()

    response = await call_next(request)

    duration = time.time() - start_time
    comparison_duration.observe(duration)

    logger.info(
        "request_completed",
        path=request.url.path,
        duration=duration,
        status_code=response.status_code
    )

    return response

    6. Cost Optimization

    Batch LLM Calls: 

    async def batch_summarize(changes: List[Change]) -> List[Summary]:
    """Reduce API calls by batching"""

    # Group similar changes
    batches = group_by_similarity(changes, threshold=0.9)

    # Single LLM call per batch
    summaries = []
    for batch in batches:
        combined_prompt = create_batch_prompt(batch)
        summary = await llm_client.complete(combined_prompt)
        summaries.extend(parse_batch_response(summary))

    return summaries

    Key Benefits of This Modular Approach:

    ✅ Independent scaling — Scale PDF processing separately from LLM summarization
    ✅ Easy testing — Mock each service independently
    ✅ Technology flexibility — Swap PyMuPDF for another parser without touching other modules
    ✅ Cost control — Caching reduces redundant API calls by 80%+
    ✅ Fault isolation — If LLM service fails, PDF processing still works
    ✅ Async processing — Handle 10K+ concurrent requests
    ✅ Monitoring — Track performance of each module separately

    This architecture is production-ready and directly applicable to [Company]'s compliance automation platform!

    Q: What do you mean by "service" when you say you'll create separate services for PDF parsing, diff engine, and LLM summarization?

    Great clarification question! Let me be very specific about what I mean by "service" in this context.

    Definition of Service: A service is an independent, self-contained module that: - Runs as a separate process (or container) - Has its own API endpoint (REST or gRPC) - Can be deployed, scaled, and updated independently - Communicates with other services via network calls (HTTP/gRPC) - Has its own database/cache if needed

    Concrete Example for This PDF Comparison System:

    Service 1: PDF Parser Service 

    # pdf_parser_service/main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI, UploadFile

app = FastAPI(title="PDF Parser Service")

@app.post("/parse")
async def parse_pdf(file: UploadFile):
    """
    Input: PDF file
    Output: Structured JSON with sections, headings, metadata
    """
    doc = extract_with_pymupdf(file)
    return {
        "sections": doc.sections,
        "metadata": doc.metadata
    }
    - Runs on: http://pdf-parser:8001 - Responsibility: ONLY PDF extraction and structure detection - Deployed as: Docker container or Kubernetes pod

    Service 2: Diff Engine Service 

    # diff_engine_service/main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI(title="Diff Engine Service")

@app.post("/compare")
async def compare_documents(doc1: dict, doc2: dict):
    """
    Input: Two structured documents (from PDF Parser)
    Output: List of changes (added/modified/removed sections)
    """
    embeddings1 = generate_embeddings(doc1)
    embeddings2 = generate_embeddings(doc2)

    matches = find_matches(embeddings1, embeddings2)

    return {
        "modified": matches.modified,
        "added": matches.added,
        "removed": matches.removed
    }
    - Runs on: http://diff-engine:8002 - Responsibility: ONLY semantic comparison and change detection - Has its own: Vector database (Qdrant) for embeddings

    Service 3: LLM Summarizer Service 

    # summarizer_service/main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI

app = FastAPI(title="Summarizer Service")

@app.post("/summarize")
async def summarize_changes(changes: dict):
    """
    Input: List of changes from Diff Engine
    Output: Human-readable summary
    """
    summary = await llm_client.generate_summary(changes)

    return {
        "executive_summary": summary.executive,
        "detailed_changes": summary.details
    }
    - Runs on: http://summarizer:8003 - Responsibility: ONLY LLM-based summarization - Has its own: Rate limiter, LLM API client

    Main API Gateway (Orchestrator) 

    # main_api/main.py
from fastapi import FastAPI
import httpx

app = FastAPI(title="Document Comparison API")

@app.post("/compare")
async def compare_pdfs(pdf1: UploadFile, pdf2: UploadFile):
    """
    Orchestrates calls to all 3 services
    """
    async with httpx.AsyncClient() as client:
        # Step 1: Parse both PDFs
        doc1 = await client.post(
            "http://pdf-parser:8001/parse",
            files={"file": pdf1}
        )
        doc2 = await client.post(
            "http://pdf-parser:8001/parse",
            files={"file": pdf2}
        )

        # Step 2: Find differences
        diff = await client.post(
            "http://diff-engine:8002/compare",
            json={"doc1": doc1.json(), "doc2": doc2.json()}
        )

        # Step 3: Summarize changes
        summary = await client.post(
            "http://summarizer:8003/summarize",
            json=diff.json()
        )

        return summary.json()
    - Runs on: http://api-gateway:8000 (public-facing) - Responsibility: Request routing and orchestration

    Why This is "Service-Oriented":

    1. Independent Deployment 

    # docker-compose.yml
services:
  pdf-parser:
    build: ./pdf_parser_service
    ports:
      - "8001:8001"
    deploy:
      replicas: 3  # Scale independently

  diff-engine:
    build: ./diff_engine_service
    ports:
      - "8002:8002"
    deploy:
      replicas: 2

  summarizer:
    build: ./summarizer_service
    ports:
      - "8003:8003"
    deploy:
      replicas: 5  # Scale based on LLM load

    2. Independent Scaling - If PDF parsing is slow → scale only PDF Parser service (add more replicas) - If LLM calls are expensive → scale only Summarizer service - Each service scales based on its own bottleneck

    3. Technology Flexibility - Want to switch from PyMuPDF to PDFPlumber? → Change only PDF Parser service - Want to use a different LLM? → Change only Summarizer service - Other services don't need to know or care

    4. Fault Isolation - If Summarizer service crashes → PDF Parser and Diff Engine still work - Can return partial results or retry only the failed service

    Contrast with Current Monolithic Approach:

    Current (Non-Modular): 

    main.py (1 file, 500 lines)
├── extract_pdf()
├── compute_embeddings()
├── find_matches()
├── summarize_with_llm()
└── main()
    - Everything runs in one process - Can't scale individual components - One bug crashes the entire system - Hard to test individual parts

    Service-Oriented (Modular): 

    api-gateway/          (orchestration)
pdf-parser-service/   (independent service)
diff-engine-service/  (independent service)
summarizer-service/   (independent service)
    - Each runs in separate process/container - Scale components independently - Fault isolation - Easy to test each service separately

    Real-World Example from My Experience:

    In the Calming AI project I built: - Main FastAPI app (orchestrator) on port 8000 - Crisis detection service (separate microservice) - Video recommendation service (separate microservice) - TTS service (separate microservice with ElevenLabs fallback)

    Each service could be deployed, scaled, and monitored independently — this is exactly what I mean by "service-oriented architecture."

    Key Takeaway: A service = independent deployable unit with its own API, not just a Python function or class. This enables horizontal scaling, fault isolation, and independent development cycles — critical for production systems at [Company]'s scale.

    Does this clarify what I mean by creating separate services?

    Q: The interviewer expressed concern about your heavy dependency on LLMs for code generation during the interview. How would you address this concern and demonstrate your understanding of the code you write?

    This is a very fair concern, and I want to address it directly because I completely agree with your philosophy of being in the driving seat while using AI tools.

    My Clarification on LLM Usage:

    What I Actually Do (vs. What It May Have Looked Like):

    1. I Use LLMs as a "Smart Autocomplete," Not an Architect - Today's interview context: I was under time pressure (45 minutes) to implement a complete PDF comparison system from scratch - My approach: I used Claude to accelerate boilerplate code generation (imports, class structures, basic parsing logic) so I could focus on the algorithmic decisions - Key point: Every architectural decision was mine: - Using PyMuPDF for font metadata extraction - Semantic chunking at section boundaries - Similarity thresholds (0.7, 0.95) for matching - Batch LLM calls to reduce costs - Modular service architecture

    2. I Debug and Validate Everything - What happened in the interview: The LLM-generated code had bugs (section_match not defined, section_difference errors) - What I did: I immediately debugged these issues because I understood: - The data flow between functions - Python scope and variable definitions - The alignment algorithm logic - This proves: I'm not blindly copy-pasting — I'm actively reviewing and fixing the code

    3. I Architect First, Then Generate - My actual workflow: 1. Design the system architecture (3 services: PDF Parser, Diff Engine, Summarizer) 2. Define the algorithm (semantic chunking → embedding → similarity matching → LLM summarization) 3. Use LLM to generate implementation of the design I already specified 4. Review, debug, and optimize the generated code

    Evidence from My Real Projects:

    Calming AI Project (Production System I Built): - Architecture decisions I made (not LLM): - 5-layer intent pipeline with LLM primary + keyword fallback - Dual FAISS-HNSW RAG with symptom-aware score boosting - 3-way VAD decision logic (ACCEPT/REJECT/AMBIGUOUS) - Rolling conversation summaries every 5-10 turns - Multi-provider TTS with ElevenLabs fallback - Results: 95% intent detection accuracy, sub-second latency, 90+ similarity scores - These metrics prove: The system works because of thoughtful engineering, not just LLM-generated code

    [Company] E-commerce ML Project: - I designed and trained XGBoost models for next-purchase prediction - I engineered 50+ ETL pipelines with custom feature engineering - I achieved 98%+ data quality and 5% forecasting improvement - LLMs didn't exist when I did this work (2021-2023) — this was pure engineering

    How I Would Work at [Company]:

    My Development Process:

    Step 1: Understand Requirements Deeply 

    Example: "Build a regulatory document change tracker"
↓
My questions:
- What document formats? (PDF, Word, XML?)
- What change granularity? (section-level, sentence-level?)
- What compliance standards? (FDA 21 CFR Part 11, ISO 13485?)
- What scale? (10 docs/day or 10,000?)
- What latency requirements? (real-time or batch?)

    Step 2: Design Architecture 

    My design (on whiteboard/paper):
┌─────────────────────────────────────┐
│ FastAPI Gateway (auth, rate limit)  │
└──────┬──────────────┬───────────────┘
       │              │
       ▼              ▼
┌──────────────┐  ┌──────────────┐
│ PDF Parser   │  │ Diff Engine  │
│ (PyMuPDF)    │  │ (embeddings) │
└──────────────┘  └──────────────┘
       │              │
       └──────┬───────┘
              ▼
       ┌──────────────┐
       │ Summarizer   │
       │ (Groq LLM)   │
       └──────────────┘

    Step 3: Implement with LLM Assistance 

    # I write the interface first (my design):
class PDFParserService:
    def extract_structured_content(self, pdf_path: str) -> StructuredDocument:
        """
        Extract sections with font metadata.
        Returns: StructuredDocument with sections, headings, metadata
        """
        pass  # LLM can fill this in based on my spec

# Then I review and optimize the LLM-generated implementation

    Step 4: Test, Debug, Optimize 

    # I write the tests (my understanding):
def test_section_extraction():
    doc = parser.extract("test.pdf")
    assert len(doc.sections) > 0
    assert doc.sections[0].heading is not None
    assert doc.sections[0].font_size >= 12

# I profile and optimize (my expertise):
@profile
def extract_structured_content(self, pdf_path: str):
    # Add caching, batch processing, error handling
    ...

    Why This Approach Works:

    ✅ Speed: LLMs handle boilerplate → I focus on hard problems
    ✅ Quality: I review every line → catch bugs and optimize
    ✅ Understanding: I design the architecture → I know how it works
    ✅ Maintainability: I write tests and docs → future-proof

    My Commitment to [Company]:

    I will: - Architect all systems myself (service design, data flow, API contracts) - Use LLMs for acceleration (boilerplate, repetitive code, documentation) - Review and understand every line of code before deployment - Write comprehensive tests to ensure correctness - Document design decisions for team knowledge sharing - Be in the driving seat — LLMs are my tools, not my brain

    I will NOT: - Blindly copy-paste LLM-generated code - Deploy code I don't understand - Rely on LLMs for architectural decisions - Skip code review or testing

    Final Thought:

    The fact that I debugged the LLM-generated code in real-time during this interview proves I understand what I'm doing. I caught the bugs, fixed them, and explained the algorithm — that's the mark of an engineer who uses tools effectively rather than being dependent on them.

    I'm excited about [Company]'s mission to bring intelligence to compliance workflows, and I'm confident I can contribute as a thoughtful, independent engineer who happens to use modern tools efficiently.

    Does this address your concern? I'm happy to discuss further or demonstrate my understanding of any specific part of the code we wrote today.

    Q: What is the difference between three dot (...) and two dot (..) in React/Node.js?

    Great question! These are JavaScript operators with very different purposes:

    1. Three Dots (...) — Spread/Rest Operator

    Use Case 1: Spread Operator (Expanding Arrays/Objects) 

    // Array spreading
const arr1 = [1, 2, 3];
const arr2 = [...arr1, 4, 5];  // [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]

// Object spreading (very common in React)
const user = { name: "John", age: 25 };
const updatedUser = { ...user, age: 26 };  
// { name: "John", age: 26 }

    Common in React for State Updates: 

    // Immutable state update
const [formData, setFormData] = useState({ name: "", email: "" });

// Update only email, keep name unchanged
setFormData({ ...formData, email: "new@email.com" });

    Use Case 2: Rest Operator (Collecting Arguments) 

    // Function parameters
function sum(...numbers) {
  return numbers.reduce((a, b) => a + b, 0);
}
sum(1, 2, 3, 4);  // 10

// Destructuring
const { name, ...otherProps } = props;
// name = "John", otherProps = { age: 25, city: "NYC" }

    2. Two Dots (..) — Relative Path Navigation

    Use Case: File System Paths (Import Statements) 

    // Current directory structure:
// src/
//   components/
//     Button.jsx
//   utils/
//     helpers.js

// In Button.jsx, import from utils:
import { formatDate } from '../utils/helpers.js';
//                          ^^
//                          Go up one directory level

// Go up two levels:
import { config } from '../../config.js';
//                      ^^^^
//                      Go up two directory levels

    Path Navigation Rules: - . = current directory - .. = parent directory (up one level) - ../.. = grandparent directory (up two levels)

    Real-World React Example:

    // Project structure:
// src/
//   pages/
//     Dashboard/
//       Dashboard.jsx
//   components/
//     shared/
//       Button.jsx
//   utils/
//     api.js

// In Dashboard.jsx:
import Button from '../../components/shared/Button';
//                  ^^                ← up to src/
//                    ^^              ← up to pages/
import { fetchData } from '../../utils/api';

// Using spread operator in the same file:
const [state, setState] = useState({ loading: false, data: [] });

const updateData = (newData) => {
  setState({ ...state, data: newData });
  //         ^^^^^^^^ spread operator
};

    Key Differences Summary:

    Feature ... (Three Dots) .. (Two Dots)
    Purpose Spread/Rest operator Path navigation
    Context JavaScript syntax File system paths
    Usage Arrays, objects, function args Import/require statements
    Example {...obj} '../utils/file.js'

    Common Interview Follow-up:

    Q: Why use spread operator instead of direct mutation? 

    // ❌ BAD (mutates state directly)
state.data.push(newItem);
setState(state);  // React won't detect change!

// ✅ GOOD (creates new object)
setState({ ...state, data: [...state.data, newItem] });
// React detects new reference → triggers re-render

    In My Calming AI Project: 

    // I used spread operator for conversation state updates:
const [conversationHistory, setConversationHistory] = useState([]);

const addMessage = (message) => {
  setConversationHistory([
    ...conversationHistory,  // ← spread existing messages
    { role: "user", content: message }
  ]);
};

// And relative imports for modular structure:
import { VoiceInput } from '../../components/VoiceInput';
import { ChatDisplay } from '../../components/ChatDisplay';

    This distinction is fundamental to React development — spread operator for immutable state updates, relative paths for module imports!

    Role: GenAI NLP Engineer¶

    Interview 1 (Possible Company context: [Company])¶

    Q: Describe a production-ready system you designed that failed, explain the root cause, and how you fixed it.

    • One example is from my work on the Knowledge GPT (KGPT) project, where we built an enterprise RAG-based AI assistant for knowledge retrieval.
    • After deploying the initial version, we noticed that the system sometimes returned irrelevant or low-quality answers, especially when users asked complex or ambiguous questions.
    • The root cause was mainly due to limitations in the retrieval pipeline—our initial semantic search setup with Elasticsearch was not retrieving the most contextually relevant document chunks, which led to poor grounding for the LLM and increased hallucinations.
    • To fix this, we migrated our vector search backend from Elasticsearch to OpenSearch, which offered better support for dense vector retrieval and scalability.
    • We also improved our document chunking and embedding strategies, making sure that the context provided to the LLM was more focused and relevant.
    • Additionally, we implemented advanced retrieval optimization techniques, such as re-ranking retrieved results using embedding similarity and adding feedback loops for continuous improvement.
    • After these changes, the system’s answer quality and relevance improved significantly, and user satisfaction increased.
    • This experience taught me the importance of robust retrieval design and continuous monitoring in production AI systems.

    Q: Explain the end-to-end RAG pipeline architecture you built—what components did you add and how did you proceed?

    • I designed and implemented the full Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) pipeline for our enterprise Knowledge GPT assistant.
    • The pipeline starts with document ingestion, where we collect and process various internal documents (PDFs, web pages, etc.).
    • Each document is split into smaller chunks, and we generate embeddings for each chunk using OpenAI or Azure OpenAI models (1536-dimensional vectors).
    • We also summarize large chunks if needed, and attach metadata like chunk ID, creation date, and source information.
    • All chunks, along with their embeddings and metadata, are bulk ingested into OpenSearch using the Bulk API, with retry logic for reliability.
    • For search, we use a hybrid retrieval approach:
    • Lexical search (BM25) for exact keyword matches.
    • Semantic search (KNN vector search) for meaning-based retrieval.
    • We combine both using Reciprocal Rank Fusion (RRF) to get the most relevant results.
    • The top-ranked chunks are used to build the context for the LLM prompt.
    • We dynamically generate prompts by injecting the retrieved context, user query, and other parameters (like language or persona) into a prompt template.
    • The prompt is sent to the LLM (via Azure OpenAI or OpenAI API) to generate the final answer.
    • The system supports localization, rich media, and can handle multiple languages.
    • We also implemented monitoring, evaluation, and feedback loops to continuously improve retrieval quality and reduce hallucinations.
    • All APIs are exposed via FastAPI for easy integration with other enterprise systems on AWS or Azure.

    Q: How do you ensure efficient token usage and control costs when multiple teams are using a high-token LLM model in a large project?

    • First, I would set clear usage guidelines and best practices for prompt engineering, such as keeping prompts concise, removing unnecessary context, and limiting the number of retrieved document chunks.
    • I would implement automated monitoring and logging of token usage per API call, user, and team, using correlation IDs and audit logs. This helps identify heavy users or inefficient patterns.
    • I would enforce token limits at the API level, such as maximum input and output token sizes, and reject or truncate requests that exceed these limits.
    • For batch or background jobs, I would schedule them during off-peak hours and set stricter token quotas.
    • I would regularly review usage reports and share them with teams, highlighting areas for optimization and providing feedback.
    • I would encourage the use of smaller, cheaper models for non-critical tasks or initial drafts, reserving high-token models like Opus 4.7 for only the most important or complex queries.
    • Secrets and API keys would be managed securely using AWS Secrets Manager, and access would be controlled via JWT authentication and authorization.
    • Finally, I would set up fallback mechanisms and content filters to avoid unnecessary token consumption on invalid or harmful requests.

    This approach ensures efficient token usage, controls costs, and maintains system performance across the organization.

    Q: Why did you choose SetFit for fine-tuning instead of directly using LLMs? How do you justify this choice?

    • I chose SetFit for fine-tuning intent classification because it is highly efficient for few-shot learning and works well with limited labeled data, which is common in enterprise scenarios.
    • SetFit uses sentence transformers to generate embeddings and then trains a lightweight classification head, making it much faster and less resource-intensive compared to full LLM fine-tuning.
    • It does not require GPU-heavy infrastructure for inference, so it is cost-effective and easy to deploy at scale, especially as a SageMaker endpoint.
    • SetFit achieves strong accuracy with fewer training examples and supports quick iteration, which is important for rapidly evolving intent taxonomies.
    • In our UIDS project, SetFit allowed us to reach ~89% accuracy across 49 languages, handling multilingual data efficiently.
    • Directly fine-tuning large LLMs is much more expensive, slower, and often unnecessary for intent classification tasks where sentence-level understanding is enough.
    • For more complex tasks or generative use cases, we do use LLMs, but for intent detection, SetFit provides the best balance of speed, accuracy, and cost.

    Q: How would you justify not using GPT-4 (or similar large LLMs) for intent classification, especially to a non-technical or finance stakeholder?

    • Using GPT-4 or similar large LLMs for intent classification is technically possible, but it is not practical for our use case due to several reasons:
      • Cost: GPT-4 is much more expensive to run, especially at high volumes (like 250 TPS in production). SetFit and similar models are lightweight and much cheaper to deploy and scale.
      • Latency: LLMs like GPT-4 have higher response times, which can impact user experience in real-time systems. SetFit provides fast, low-latency predictions.
      • Overkill for Task: Intent classification is a sentence-level task that does not require the full generative power of GPT-4. Simpler models like SetFit are optimized for this and perform very well.
      • Multilingual Support: SetFit supports 80+ languages out-of-the-box, which was a key requirement for [Company]. GPT-4’s multilingual capabilities are strong, but not as cost-effective or easy to manage for this specific use case.
      • Resource Efficiency: SetFit can run on standard CPU infrastructure, while GPT-4 typically requires more powerful (and expensive) hardware.
      • Data Privacy: With SetFit, we have more control over data residency and privacy, as the model can be fully managed within our cloud environment.
    • In summary, for intent classification, SetFit gives us the best balance of accuracy, speed, cost, and language coverage. We reserve GPT-4 for more complex, generative, or open-ended tasks where its capabilities are truly needed. This approach is both technically and financially responsible for the business.

    Q: Why would you choose LangGraph over LangChain for pipeline orchestration? What are the advantages?

    • LangGraph is designed for building complex, modular, and stateful workflows using a graph-based approach, where each step is a node and edges define the order and branching.
    • It allows for easy orchestration of multi-step, conditional, and branching pipelines, which is ideal for advanced RAG, data labeling, or agentic AI workflows.
    • With LangGraph, you can:
    • Visualize and manage the flow of tasks, making debugging and maintenance easier.
    • Add or modify steps (nodes) without breaking the whole pipeline, supporting extensibility and modularity.
    • Handle conditional logic (e.g., skip translation if not needed) and parallel execution natively.
    • Track state and data as it moves through each node, improving transparency and traceability.
    • In my past projects (like UIDS RAG Data Labelling), LangGraph helped us modularize the workflow, add logging, and support cloud integration (S3, secrets management) efficiently.
    • LangChain is great for chaining simple LLM calls, but for complex, production-grade pipelines with many steps and conditional logic, LangGraph provides better structure, flexibility, and control.

    Q: How would you design a payment gateway system to handle sudden spikes in traffic (e.g., 50,000 TPS), ensuring scalability, low latency, and cost efficiency?

    • I would use a cloud-native, microservices-based architecture deployed on Kubernetes (EKS) to support both horizontal and vertical scaling.
    • For horizontal scaling, I would enable Kubernetes Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) to automatically add or remove pods based on CPU, memory, or custom metrics (like request rate or latency).
    • For vertical scaling, I would use Cluster Autoscaler or Karpenter to dynamically adjust the number and size of nodes in the cluster, ensuring enough resources during peak loads and scaling down during low traffic to save costs.
    • I would use AWS Application Load Balancer (ALB) or Network Load Balancer (NLB) to distribute incoming traffic evenly across healthy pods, ensuring high availability and low latency.
    • To handle sudden spikes (like Big Billion Days), I would implement PodDisruptionBudgets and multi-AZ node groups for resilience, and use readiness/liveness probes to ensure only healthy pods receive traffic.
    • For cost control, I would set up auto-scaling policies with upper limits, use spot instances for non-critical workloads, and monitor resource usage with CloudWatch and Grafana to optimize infrastructure.
    • I would use distributed tracing (e.g., AWS X-Ray with correlation IDs) to monitor end-to-end latency and quickly identify bottlenecks.
    • For backend services (like payment processing, fraud checks), I would use asynchronous processing and circuit breakers to prevent cascading failures.
    • Secrets and credentials would be managed securely using IAM Roles for Service Accounts (IRSA) and AWS Secrets Manager.
    • Finally, I would regularly test the system with load testing tools to validate scaling policies and latency under high TPS scenarios.

    This approach ensures the payment gateway can handle massive, unpredictable spikes in traffic with minimal latency and optimal cost efficiency.

    Q: How will you implement batching strategies, monitor latency (like p95), and respond to latency issues in a high-throughput payment system?

    • For batching, I would implement asynchronous processing queues (like AWS SQS or Kafka) to collect incoming payment requests and process them in batches, which helps reduce load on backend services and improves throughput.
      • Batch size and interval would be tuned based on real-time traffic and service response times.
      • For critical real-time payments, I would keep batch sizes small or use micro-batching to balance speed and efficiency.
    • To monitor latency, I would:
      • Collect detailed metrics (p50, p95, p99 latency) using CloudWatch Metrics and distributed tracing with AWS X-Ray.
      • Set up dashboards in Grafana to visualize latency trends and identify spikes or bottlenecks.
      • Use correlation IDs in logs and traces to track individual requests through the system for root cause analysis.
    • If p95 latency increases beyond a set threshold:
      • I would trigger auto-scaling policies to add more pods or nodes automatically.
      • Investigate logs and traces to identify slow components or overloaded services.
      • Adjust batch sizes or processing intervals dynamically to reduce queue buildup.
      • If needed, enable circuit breakers or fallback mechanisms to protect critical paths.
    • Regular load testing and chaos engineering would be used to validate that batching and scaling strategies keep latency within acceptable limits, even during peak events.

    This approach ensures efficient batching, real-time latency monitoring, and rapid response to any performance issues, keeping the payment system reliable and fast.

    Q: How would you design an AI-driven agent to monitor and manage latency spikes in real time, especially during unexpected traffic surges, using observability tools and automation?

    • I would build a custom AI-powered monitoring agent integrated with our observability stack (CloudWatch, X-Ray, Grafana) and the MCP (Model Context Protocol) framework.
    • The agent would continuously analyze real-time logs, metrics (like p95 latency, error rates), and distributed traces pushed to Grafana and CloudWatch.
    • It would use anomaly detection algorithms (e.g., statistical thresholds, ML models) to identify sudden spikes in latency or error rates, even during off-peak hours or unplanned sales events.
    • When the agent detects abnormal patterns (like a sudden jump in p95 latency or a surge in failed transactions), it would:
      • Automatically trigger alerts via SNS, Slack, or PagerDuty to notify the engineering team.
      • Correlate logs and traces using correlation IDs to pinpoint the exact service/component causing the bottleneck (e.g., payment gateway, adapter, backend).
      • Suggest or initiate automated remediation actions, such as scaling up pods, restarting unhealthy services, or rerouting traffic to healthy nodes.
      • Log all incidents and actions for audit and post-mortem analysis (using CloudTrail).
    • The agent could also provide recommendations for future prevention, like adjusting auto-scaling thresholds or optimizing batch sizes.
    • This AI-driven approach ensures proactive detection and response to latency issues, minimizes downtime, and maintains a smooth payment experience even during unexpected traffic spikes.

    This solution leverages both AI and observability best practices, as described in my MCP design experience, to deliver real-time, intelligent monitoring and automated incident management.

    Q: How will you monitor your AI systems in production?

    • For monitoring AI systems in production, I would implement a comprehensive observability stack covering logging, metrics, and distributed tracing, as described in my MCP (Model Context Protocol) design experience.
    • Logging: All AI service logs (inference requests, errors, model decisions) are aggregated using CloudWatch Logs and visualized in Grafana. Logs are structured (JSON) and include correlation IDs for end-to-end traceability.
    • Metrics: Key metrics like model latency (p50/p95/p99), throughput, error rates, and resource usage (CPU, memory) are collected via CloudWatch Metrics and displayed on unified Grafana dashboards. Custom business metrics (e.g., prediction confidence, drift scores) are also tracked.
    • Distributed Tracing: Using AWS X-Ray and correlation IDs, I enable tracing of each request through the entire AI pipeline—from API gateway to model inference and downstream services. This helps quickly identify bottlenecks or failures.
    • Alerting: Automated alerts are set up for anomalies (e.g., latency spikes, error rate increases, model drift) using SNS, Slack, or PagerDuty, so the team is notified in real time.
    • Audit Logging: All model predictions and actions are logged for compliance and post-mortem analysis using CloudTrail.
    • Automated Evaluation: I also implement continuous evaluation pipelines to monitor model accuracy, data drift, and output quality, triggering retraining or rollback if needed.
    • This unified approach ensures that AI systems are reliable, transparent, and easy to debug in production, supporting both technical and business requirements.

    Q: How will you measure hallucination rate, model drift, and failure rates for your AI system in production?

    • To measure hallucination rate, I would implement an automated evaluation pipeline that checks if the AI’s answers are factually correct and relevant to the input question.
      • For example, in my Knowledge GPT project, we used a combination of LLM-based evaluation (like GPT-4o) and embedding-based semantic similarity to compare the AI’s answer with the ground truth or source documents.
      • If the answer does not match the expected context or contains unsupported information, it is flagged as a hallucination.
      • The hallucination rate is calculated as the percentage of responses flagged as hallucinated over the total responses.
    • For model drift, I would monitor changes in model predictions over time using statistical tests and track input data distributions.
      • I would compare recent inference data and outputs with historical data to detect significant shifts.
      • Automated drift detection tools or custom scripts can alert if the model’s behavior changes beyond a set threshold.
    • For failure rates, I would track error metrics (like failed inferences, timeouts, or exceptions) using CloudWatch Metrics and log aggregation.
      • These metrics are visualized in Grafana dashboards and can trigger alerts if failure rates exceed acceptable limits.
    • All these metrics (hallucination rate, drift, failures) are included in regular evaluation reports, and automated pipelines upload detailed results and charts to S3 for analysis and cross-release comparison.

    This approach ensures continuous monitoring of AI quality, reliability, and stability in production.

    Q: How would you design your system if all AI/cloud services (like ChatGPT, cloud databases) suddenly became unavailable?

    • If all AI and cloud services suddenly became unavailable, I would shift to an on-premises, self-hosted architecture using open-source technologies.
    • For core business logic, I would rely on traditional rule-based systems and deterministic algorithms, ensuring all critical workflows can run without AI dependencies.
    • For data storage, I would deploy on-premises relational databases (like PostgreSQL or MySQL) and set up regular backups using local or network-attached storage.
    • For search and retrieval, I would use open-source search engines like Elasticsearch or Apache Solr, hosted on local servers.
    • For NLP or information extraction, I would use lightweight, open-source libraries (like SpaCy or NLTK) that can run locally, even if advanced LLMs are unavailable.
    • For orchestration and workflow management, I would use tools like Apache Airflow or custom Python scripts to automate batch jobs and ETL processes.
    • For monitoring and observability, I would set up local instances of Grafana and Prometheus to track system health, latency, and error rates.
    • For scaling, I would design the system to run on a cluster of physical or virtual machines, using load balancers (like HAProxy or Nginx) to distribute traffic.
    • Security would be managed with local firewalls, network segmentation, and strict access controls.
    • Regular disaster recovery drills and offline documentation would be maintained to ensure business continuity.

    This approach ensures the system remains operational, secure, and maintainable even in a scenario where all cloud and AI services are lost, leveraging open-source and on-premises solutions for resilience.

    Interview 2 (Possible Company context: [Company])¶

    Q: How do you evaluate your ranking model in the Knowledge GPT project?

    • For the Knowledge GPT (KGPT) project, we use a dedicated evaluation pipeline to measure the quality of our ranking and retrieval system.
    • The main metric we use is Mean Reciprocal Rank (MRR), which checks if the expected (ground truth) document appears in the search results and at what position.
    • For each query, we:
    • Send the query to the Search API.
    • Check if the correct document is present in the returned results and note its rank (position).
    • Calculate the reciprocal rank (1/rank) for each query. If the document is not found, the score is 0.
    • We then average these reciprocal ranks across all queries to get the MRR score.
    • We also track Precision@1, which is the fraction of queries where the correct document is ranked first.
    • The evaluation pipeline generates detailed reports, including:
    • Per-query rank and reciprocal rank.
    • Aggregate MRR and Precision@1 for different search types (like lexical, semantic, hybrid).
    • Breakdown of found, not found, and empty results.
    • All evaluation results are stored in S3 for release comparison and tracking improvements over time.
    • This process helps us monitor and improve the ranking quality of our RAG system in a systematic way.

    Q: Why use LLM-based evaluation if you already have ground truth data?

    • Even though we have ground truth data, we use LLM-based evaluation because not all aspects of answer quality can be measured by simple string matching or exact document ID checks.
    • The LLM (like GPT-4o) acts as a judge to assess deeper qualities of the generated answer, such as:
    • Factual correctness: The LLM compares the generated answer to the ground truth and scores how factually accurate it is, not just if it matches word-for-word.
    • Semantic similarity: Sometimes, the answer can be correct but phrased differently. The LLM can judge if the meaning matches, even if the wording is different.
    • Reference health: The LLM checks if the answer properly cites or uses the right references from the context.
    • Answer relevance: The LLM evaluates if the answer actually addresses the question, covering all required parts.
    • Context utilization: The LLM checks if the answer uses the provided context effectively, not just copying or ignoring it.
    • These metrics are combined using weighted scores to give a more complete and nuanced evaluation of the model’s performance.
    • This approach helps us catch issues like hallucinations, partial answers, or irrelevant content, which simple ground truth checks might miss.
    • In summary, LLM-based evaluation gives us a more human-like, comprehensive assessment of answer quality, beyond what ground truth alone can provide.

    Q: How can you compare LLM-generated answers to ground truth using traditional NLP methods, without using LLM-as-a-judge?

    • Before LLM-based evaluation, we used several traditional NLP techniques to compare generated answers with ground truth:
    • String Matching: Directly compare the generated answer with the ground truth using exact or partial string match. This is simple but often too strict, as it does not handle paraphrasing.
    • Token Overlap Metrics: Use metrics like BLEU, ROUGE, or F1-score to measure the overlap of words or n-grams between the generated answer and the ground truth. These metrics are common in text summarization and translation tasks.
    • Semantic Similarity: Compute the cosine similarity between embeddings of the generated answer and the ground truth. We can use models like Sentence Transformers (e.g., BERT, SBERT) to get sentence embeddings and then calculate similarity. This helps capture meaning even if the wording is different.
    • Jaccard Similarity: Measure the intersection over union of unique words or tokens in both answers.
    • Levenshtein Distance: Calculate the minimum number of edits needed to change one answer into the other, which gives a sense of how close the answers are.
    • In practice, semantic similarity using embeddings is the most robust, as it can handle paraphrased or reworded answers better than pure string-based methods.
    • For our projects, we often use Sentence Transformers to encode both the generated answer and the ground truth, then compute cosine similarity. If the similarity score is above a certain threshold (e.g., 0.8), we consider the answer correct.
    • These methods are fast, interpretable, and do not require LLM inference, but they may miss subtle errors or hallucinations that only a human or LLM judge can catch.
    • Overall, combining these traditional metrics gives a good baseline for automated answer evaluation when ground truth is available.

    Q: Which evaluation metric is best for comparing LLM-generated answers to ground truth, and why?

    • In my experience, the best option for comparing LLM-generated answers to ground truth is semantic similarity using embeddings.
    • This approach uses models like Sentence Transformers or OpenAI embeddings to convert both the generated answer and the ground truth into high-dimensional vectors, then calculates the cosine similarity between them.
